confidence-interval.oneLine: 一套會生出區間的規則。95% 是這套規則的長期命中率，不是眼前這一個區間的機率。
confidence-interval.example: Neyman 1937 自己問：對已經算出來的那一個區間，能不能說真值落在裡面的機率是 95%？他的答案是不能。

related.oneLine: 效果有多大，用一把不隨樣本數伸縮的尺量出來。
related.example: 1988 年的阿斯匹靈試驗 p 小於 0.00001，效果量換算成相關係數只有 0.03 左右。同一批數字，一個像鐵證，一個小到幾乎看不見。

en.oneLine: How large the effect is, on a scale that does not stretch with the sample.
en.example: The 1988 aspirin trial had p below 0.00001 and an effect size of about r = 0.03. Same numbers: one looks like proof, the other is nearly invisible.

related.oneLine: 假設虛無為真，得到「像這樣或更極端」的結果的機率。
related.example: 八杯茶全挑對，純靠猜的機率是 70 分之 1，約 0.014。這個 0.014 就是 p。

en.oneLine: The probability of a result at least as extreme as the one you got, if the null were true.
en.example: Picking all four milk-first cups by luck alone has probability 1 in 70, about 0.014. That 0.014 is the p value.

related.oneLine: 同一個統計量在反覆抽樣下會長成的樣子。p 值與信賴區間都是從它身上量出來的。
related.example: 品茶實驗的 70 種挑法就是一個抽樣分佈：全部列出來，再數有幾種跟實際結果一樣極端。

en.oneLine: The shape a statistic takes under repeated sampling. Both p values and confidence intervals are read off it.
en.example: The 70 ways of picking four cups out of eight are a sampling distribution: list them all, then count how many are as extreme as what actually happened.

en.oneLine: A rule for producing intervals. The 95% is the rule's long-run hit rate, not the probability for the one interval in front of you.
en.example: Neyman asked it himself in 1937: of an interval already computed, may we say the true value lies inside it with probability 95%? His answer was no.

neyman1937ConfidenceIntervals.author: Jerzy Neyman
neyman1937ConfidenceIntervals.title: Outline of a Theory of Statistical Estimation Based on the Classical Theory of Probability
neyman1937ConfidenceIntervals.container: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London, Series A, vol. 231, pp.333-380
neyman1937ConfidenceIntervals.locator: p.349（信賴區間的頻率解釋：長期而言，照這套規則做的陳述有 100a% 是對的；緊接著自問「對這一個已經算出來的區間，能不能說參數落在裡面的機率是 a」，並自答不能）。
neyman1937ConfidenceIntervals.quote: It follows that if the practical statistician applies permanently the rules (a), (b) and (c) ... in the long run he will be correct in about 99 per cent. of all cases. ... Can we say that in this particular case the probability of the true value of [the parameter] falling between 1 and 2 is equal to [the confidence coefficient]? The answer is obviously in the negative. The parameter ... is an unknown constant and no probability statement concerning its value may be made.

en.locator: p.349 (the frequency reading of a confidence interval: in the long run the statements will be right in 100a per cent of cases; Neyman then asks whether one may say of a single computed interval that the parameter lies in it with probability a, and answers that one may not).
en.quote: It follows that if the practical statistician applies permanently the rules (a), (b) and (c) ... in the long run he will be correct in about 99 per cent. of all cases. ... Can we say that in this particular case the probability of the true value of [the parameter] falling between 1 and 2 is equal to [the confidence coefficient]? The answer is obviously in the negative. The parameter ... is an unknown constant and no probability statement concerning its value may be made.

cohen1988PowerAnalysis.author: Jacob Cohen
cohen1988PowerAnalysis.title: Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences
cohen1988PowerAnalysis.locator: §2.2「The Effect Size Index: d」，pp.24-27（小 d=.2、中 d=.5、大 d=.8 的約定，以及各自的真實對照物：雙胞胎與非雙胞胎的平均智商差距約當小效果，14 歲與 18 歲女孩的平均身高差距約當中效果，博士學位持有者與大一新生的平均智商差距約當大效果）。Cohen 在同節自承這組對應是任意的。
cohen1988PowerAnalysis.quote: MEDIUM EFFECT SIZE: d = .5. A medium effect size is conceived as one large enough to be visible to the naked eye. ... We are thus reminded of the arbitrariness of this assignment of quantitative operational definitions to qualitative adjectives.

en.locator: Section 2.2, "The Effect Size Index: d", pp.24-27 (the small d=.2, medium d=.5, large d=.8 conventions, each anchored to something real: the mean IQ gap between twins and non-twins is about a small effect, the mean height difference between 14- and 18-year-old girls about a medium one, the IQ gap between PhD holders and college freshmen about a large one). Cohen calls the mapping arbitrary in the same section.
en.quote: MEDIUM EFFECT SIZE: d = .5. A medium effect size is conceived as one large enough to be visible to the naked eye. ... We are thus reminded of the arbitrariness of this assignment of quantitative operational definitions to qualitative adjectives.

rosenthal1990SoftPsychology.author: Robert Rosenthal
rosenthal1990SoftPsychology.title: How Are We Doing in Soft Psychology?
rosenthal1990SoftPsychology.locator: 以二元效果量顯示法（Binomial Effect Size Display, BESD）重新詮釋阿斯匹靈與心肌梗塞試驗的效果量：相關係數 r 僅約 .03，量級很小，但換算成「每若干人中避免幾次心肌梗塞」的實際人數後，效果並不能說是無足輕重。

en.locator: Reinterprets the aspirin/myocardial-infarction trial's effect size using the Binomial Effect Size Display (BESD): a correlation of r approximately .03 is small in magnitude, but translated into "heart attacks prevented per N people," the practical effect is not negligible.

steeringCommittee1988PhysiciansHealthStu.author: Steering Committee of the Physicians' Health Study Research Group
steeringCommittee1988PhysiciansHealthStu.title: Preliminary Report: Findings from the Aspirin Component of the Ongoing Physicians' Health Study
steeringCommittee1988PhysiciansHealthStu.container: New England Journal of Medicine, vol. 318, no. 4, pp.262–264
steeringCommittee1988PhysiciansHealthStu.locator: 22,071 名男性醫師隨機分派服用阿斯匹靈（11,037 人）或安慰劑（11,034 人）；追蹤期間阿斯匹靈組 104 人、安慰劑組 189 人發生心肌梗塞，相對風險約 0.56（降低 44%），因效果極端顯著而提前終止試驗。

en.locator: 22,071 male physicians randomized to aspirin (n=11,037) or placebo (n=11,034); 104 myocardial infarctions occurred in the aspirin group versus 189 in the placebo group, relative risk approximately 0.56 (a 44% reduction) — the effect was extreme enough that the trial's aspirin component was stopped early.

en.title: Why It Is Called the Null Hypothesis

multiple-comparisons.oneLine: 檢定做得夠多，光靠雜訊也會冒出「顯著」。不校正，門檻就名存實亡。
multiple-comparisons.example: 一條死掉的大西洋鮭進了 fMRI 機器，8,064 個 voxel 裡有 16 個「顯著」。做完校正，一個都不剩。

related.oneLine: 一邊看結果一邊調分析：多收幾個樣本、換個共變量、換個結果變數，直到 p 掉到門檻底下。
related.example: Simmons 等人 2011 年「證明」聽披頭四的〈When I'm Sixty-Four〉會讓人年輕一歲半（p = .040）。拿掉一個共變量，p 就跳回 .33。

en.oneLine: Adjusting the analysis while watching the result — a few more subjects, another covariate, another outcome — until p drops below the line.
en.example: Simmons and colleagues "showed" in 2011 that hearing "When I'm Sixty-Four" made people a year and a half younger (p = .040). Drop one covariate and p goes back to .33.

related.oneLine: 事前選定的門檻：長期而言，你願意讓多少比例的真虛無被錯判為顯著。
related.example: Fisher 說二十分之一是個方便的界線，如此而已。方便，而且當年好查表。

en.oneLine: A threshold fixed in advance: the share of true nulls you are willing to reject in the long run.
en.example: Fisher called one in twenty a convenient limit. Convenient, and easy to look up in a table.

related.oneLine: 在所有「顯著」的結果裡，真的有效果的佔幾成。它取決於這個領域有多少假說本來就是對的。
related.example: Ioannidis 2005 的算術就是這一條：假說多半是錯的領域，加上偏低的檢定力，「顯著」結果會有一大半是假的。

en.oneLine: Of everything that comes out "significant," how much is real. It depends on how many hypotheses in the field were true to begin with.
en.example: This is the arithmetic behind Ioannidis 2005: in a field where most hypotheses are false and power is low, most significant findings are false too.

en.oneLine: Run enough tests and noise alone will hand you a "significant" one. Without correction the threshold means nothing.
en.example: A dead Atlantic salmon went into an fMRI scanner: 16 of 8,064 voxels came out "significant." After correction, none did.

benjaminiHochberg1995FDR.author: Yoav Benjamini and Yosef Hochberg
benjaminiHochberg1995FDR.title: Controlling the False Discovery Rate: A Practical and Powerful Approach to Multiple Testing
benjaminiHochberg1995FDR.container: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B (Methodological), 57(1), pp.289-300
benjaminiHochberg1995FDR.locator: 全文（錯誤發現率 FDR 的原始提出：改控制「被拒絕的假設裡有多少比例是錯拒」的期望值，取代族系錯誤率）。DOI 10.1111/j.2517-6161.1995.tb02031.x。

en.locator: The paper as a whole (the original proposal of the false discovery rate: control the expected proportion of errors among the rejected hypotheses instead of the familywise error rate). DOI 10.1111/j.2517-6161.1995.tb02031.x.

bennett2010DeadSalmon.author: Craig M. Bennett, Abigail A. Baird, Michael B. Miller and George L. Wolford
bennett2010DeadSalmon.title: Neural Correlates of Interspecies Perspective Taking in the Post-Mortem Atlantic Salmon: An Argument For Proper Multiple Comparisons Correction
bennett2010DeadSalmon.container: Journal of Serendipitous and Unexpected Results, 1(1), pp.1-5（2009 年以海報形式發表於 Human Brain Mapping 年會）
bennett2010DeadSalmon.locator: Methods 與 Results 欄：受試者為一條約 18 吋、3.8 磅的大西洋鮭，掃描時已死亡；未校正門檻 t(131) > 3.15、p < 0.001 下，8,064 個 voxel 中有 16 個「顯著」，且群聚在鮭魚的腦腔內；改用 FDR 或族系錯誤率校正後，顯著 voxel 歸零，連放寬到 p = 0.25 都沒有。
bennett2010DeadSalmon.quote: The salmon was approximately 18 inches long, weighed 3.8 lbs, and was not alive at the time of scanning. ... Out of a search volume of 8064 voxels a total of 16 voxels were significant. ... Identical t-contrasts controlling the false discovery rate (FDR) and familywise error rate (FWER) were completed. These contrasts indicated no active voxels, even at relaxed statistical thresholds (p = 0.25).

en.locator: Methods and Results: the subject was an Atlantic salmon roughly 18 inches long and 3.8 lbs, not alive at the time of scanning; at an uncorrected threshold of t(131) > 3.15, p < 0.001, 16 of 8,064 voxels came out significant, clustered inside the brain cavity of the fish; under FDR or familywise correction nothing survived, not even at a relaxed p = 0.25.
en.quote: The salmon was approximately 18 inches long, weighed 3.8 lbs, and was not alive at the time of scanning. ... Out of a search volume of 8064 voxels a total of 16 voxels were significant. ... Identical t-contrasts controlling the false discovery rate (FDR) and familywise error rate (FWER) were completed. These contrasts indicated no active voxels, even at relaxed statistical thresholds (p = 0.25).

p-hacking.oneLine: 一邊看結果一邊調分析：多收幾個樣本、換個共變量、換個結果變數，直到 p 掉到門檻底下。

en.title: Why It Is Called the Null Hypothesis

null-hypothesis.oneLine: 被設計來承受攻擊的那個假設：設下它，是為了讓資料有機會推翻它。
null-hypothesis.example: 品茶實驗的虛無假設是「她只是在猜」。Fisher 端出八杯茶，就是為了給這個假設一次被推翻的機會。

related.oneLine: 假設虛無為真，得到「像這樣或更極端」的結果的機率。
related.example: 八杯茶全挑對，純靠猜的機率是 70 分之 1，約 0.014。這個 0.014 就是 p。

en.oneLine: The probability of a result at least as extreme as the one you got, if the null were true.
en.example: Picking all four milk-first cups by luck alone has probability 1 in 70, about 0.014. That 0.014 is the p value.

related.oneLine: 事前選定的門檻：長期而言，你願意讓多少比例的真虛無被錯判為顯著。
related.example: Fisher 說二十分之一是個方便的界線，如此而已。方便，而且當年好查表。

en.oneLine: A threshold fixed in advance: the share of true nulls you are willing to reject in the long run.
en.example: Fisher called one in twenty a convenient limit. Convenient, and easy to look up in a table.

related.oneLine: 把標籤重新洗牌，看看純屬巧合能做到多好，再看真實資料排在哪裡。
related.example: 八杯茶就是最小的置換檢定：四杯先倒奶的挑法共 70 種，全對只有 1 種。

en.oneLine: Shuffle the labels, see how well coincidence can do, then see where the real data land.
en.example: Eight cups is the smallest permutation test there is: 70 ways to choose four, exactly one of them all correct.

related.oneLine: 把 Fisher 的證據邏輯和 Neyman-Pearson 的決策邏輯焊成一套固定動作，兩邊的代價都不付。
related.example: Gigerenzer 說這套儀式沒有作者：沒有哪位統計學家願意替它辯護，整個學科卻天天執行。

en.oneLine: Fisher's evidential logic and Neyman-Pearson's decision logic welded into a fixed routine that pays neither one's price.
en.example: Gigerenzer says the ritual has no author: no statistician will defend it, and the field performs it every day.

en.oneLine: The hypothesis set up to be attacked: it exists so the data have something to argue against.
en.example: In the tea experiment the null is "she is guessing." Fisher poured eight cups precisely to give that hypothesis a chance of being overturned.

fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.author: Ronald A. Fisher
fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.title: The Design of Experiments
fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.locator: Ch. II, §5 'The Statement of Experiment', p.13（茶品實驗設定：8 杯，4 杯先加奶）；§8 'The Null Hypothesis'，p.18（1935 初版；部分刷次標為 p.19）。
fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.quote: In relation to any experiment we may speak of this hypothesis as the null hypothesis, and it should be noted that the null hypothesis is never proved or established, but is possibly disproved, in the course of experimentation. Every experiment may be said to exist only in order to give the facts a chance of disproving the null hypothesis.

en.locator: Ch. II §5 'The Statement of Experiment', p.13 (the tea-tasting setup); §8 'The Null Hypothesis', p.18 (1935 first edition; some reprints give p.19).
en.quote: In relation to any experiment we may speak of this hypothesis as the null hypothesis, and it should be noted that the null hypothesis is never proved or established, but is possibly disproved, in the course of experimentation. Every experiment may be said to exist only in order to give the facts a chance of disproving the null hypothesis.

gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullEtymology.author: Gerd Gigerenzer, Stefan Krauss, and Oliver Vitouch
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullEtymology.title: The Null Ritual: What You Always Wanted to Know About Significance Testing but Were Afraid to Ask
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullEtymology.container: In D. Kaplan (Ed.), The Sage Handbook of Quantitative Methodology for the Social Sciences, pp.391–408. Thousand Oaks, CA: Sage
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullEtymology.locator: p.392（Gigerenzer 等對 Fisher 命名「null」原意的詮釋性重述，非辭典級語源條目）。
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullEtymology.quote: Some suggest that it was authorized by the eminent statistician Sir Ronald A. Fisher, owing to the emphasis on null hypothesis testing (not to be confused with the null ritual) in his 1935 book. However, Fisher would have rejected all three ingredients of this procedure. First, null does not refer to a zero mean difference or correlation but to the hypothesis to be "nullified," which could postulate a correlation of .3, for instance.

en.locator: p.392 (Gigerenzer et al.'s interpretive account of Fisher's original intent behind the term "null"; not a dictionary-grade etymology entry).

gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.author: Gerd Gigerenzer
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.title: Mindless Statistics
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.container: The Journal of Socio-Economics, vol. 33, no. 5, pp.587–606
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.locator: p.591–592：以金屬板製造商的品管情境為例，逐步示範 Neyman–Pearson 決策邏輯（設定第一類、第二類錯誤率、依成本效益抓樣本數，「接受」不等於「相信」）如何脫胎於工業品管的驗收邏輯。
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.quote: Neyman–Pearson 檢定的典型應用是品質管制。設想一位醫療器材金屬板的製造商：她認為兩類錯誤的代價不對稱——誤報（false alarm）代價較小，漏抓故障（miss）代價較大，因此把第一類錯誤率設得很小、第二類錯誤率設得較大，並據此算出每天須抽驗的樣本數。當她「接受」機器故障這個假設時，她是依此行動、停止生產，但這不代表她相信機器真的故障。

en.locator: pp.591-592: works through a metal-plate manufacturer's quality-control scenario to show how Neyman-Pearson decision logic (setting Type I/II error rates, choosing sample size by cost-benefit trade-off, "accepting" a hypothesis without believing it) grew out of industrial acceptance-sampling logic.
en.quote: A typical application of Neyman–Pearson testing is in quality control. Imagine a manufacturer of metal plates that are used in medical instruments... She considers false alarms, that is, accepting H2 while H1 is true, to be the less serious error, and misses of malfunctioning... to be more serious... She now calculates the required sample size n of plates that must be sampled every day to test the quality of the production... When she accepts H2, she acts as if there were a malfunction and stops production, but this does not mean that she believes that H2 is true.

gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullRitual.author: Gerd Gigerenzer, Stefan Krauss, and Oliver Vitouch
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullRitual.title: The Null Ritual: What You Always Wanted to Know About Significance Testing but Were Afraid to Ask
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullRitual.container: In D. Kaplan (Ed.), The Sage Handbook of Quantitative Methodology for the Social Sciences, pp.391–408. Thousand Oaks, CA: Sage
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullRitual.locator: p.392（「虛無儀式」三步驟定義；六題是非題示範 p 值的六種常見誤讀，全部為 False）
gigerenzerKraussVitouch2004NullRitual.quote: The essence of the ritual is the following: (1) Set up a statistical null hypothesis of "no mean difference" or "zero correlation." Don't specify the predictions of your research hypothesis or of any alternative substantive hypotheses. (2) Use 5% as a convention for rejecting the null. If significant, accept your research hypothesis. (3) Always perform this procedure.

en.locator: p.392 (the three-step definition of the 'null ritual'; six true/false items demonstrating six common misreadings of the p-value, all in fact false).
en.quote: The essence of the ritual is the following: (1) Set up a statistical null hypothesis of "no mean difference" or "zero correlation." Don't specify the predictions of your research hypothesis or of any alternative substantive hypotheses. (2) Use 5% as a convention for rejecting the null. If significant, accept your research hypothesis. (3) Always perform this procedure.

wassersteinLazar2016.author: Ronald L. Wasserstein and Nicole A. Lazar
wassersteinLazar2016.title: The ASA Statement on p-Values: Context, Process, and Purpose
wassersteinLazar2016.container: The American Statistician, vol. 70, no. 2, pp.129–133
wassersteinLazar2016.locator: 六條原則列表，正文 p.131（"Principle 1" 起）
wassersteinLazar2016.quote: P-values do not measure the probability that the studied hypothesis is true, or the probability that the data were produced by random chance alone.

en.locator: The six principles, listed starting p.131 (from "Principle 1").
en.quote: P-values do not measure the probability that the studied hypothesis is true, or the probability that the data were produced by random chance alone.

type-i-and-type-ii-error.oneLine: 兩種錯法：虛無為真卻拒絕它（誤報），虛無為假卻沒拒絕（漏抓）。
type-i-and-type-ii-error.example: 醫材工廠的品管：誤報一次，白停一次機；漏抓一次，瑕疵品進了手術室。代價不同，門檻就不該一樣。

en.term: Type I and Type II error
en.oneLine: Two ways to be wrong: rejecting a true null (a false alarm), or failing to reject a false one (a miss).
en.example: Quality control on a surgical-steel line: a false alarm costs an idle machine, a miss puts a flawed plate in an operating theatre. Different costs, different thresholds.

null-hypothesis.oneLine: 被設計來承受攻擊的那個假設：設下它，是為了讓資料有機會推翻它。
null-hypothesis.example: 品茶實驗的虛無假設是「她只是在猜」。Fisher 端出八杯茶，就是為了給這個假設一次被推翻的機會。

en.oneLine: The hypothesis set up to be attacked: it exists so the data have something to argue against.
en.example: In the tea experiment the null is "she is guessing." Fisher poured eight cups precisely to give that hypothesis a chance of being overturned.

statistical-power.oneLine: 效果真的存在時，這個研究抓得到它的機率。
statistical-power.example: Cohen 在 1962 年算過：那年頭的心理學研究要抓一個中等大小的效果，平均只有 .48 的機會，比丟銅板還差一點。

en.oneLine: The probability that a study will catch an effect that is really there.
en.example: Cohen worked it out in 1962: to catch a medium effect, the psychology studies of the day averaged a .48 chance. Slightly worse than a coin toss.

en.title: Why It Is Called the Null Hypothesis

p-hacking.oneLine: 一邊看結果一邊調分析：多收幾個樣本、換個共變量、換個結果變數，直到 p 掉到門檻底下。
p-hacking.example: Simmons 等人 2011 年「證明」聽披頭四的〈When I'm Sixty-Four〉會讓人年輕一歲半（p = .040）。拿掉一個共變量，p 就跳回 .33。

related.oneLine: 檢定做得夠多，光靠雜訊也會冒出「顯著」。不校正，門檻就名存實亡。
related.example: 一條死掉的大西洋鮭進了 fMRI 機器，8,064 個 voxel 裡有 16 個「顯著」。做完校正，一個都不剩。

en.oneLine: Run enough tests and noise alone will hand you a "significant" one. Without correction the threshold means nothing.
en.example: A dead Atlantic salmon went into an fMRI scanner: 16 of 8,064 voxels came out "significant." After correction, none did.

related.oneLine: 事前選定的門檻：長期而言，你願意讓多少比例的真虛無被錯判為顯著。
related.example: Fisher 說二十分之一是個方便的界線，如此而已。方便，而且當年好查表。

en.oneLine: A threshold fixed in advance: the share of true nulls you are willing to reject in the long run.
en.example: Fisher called one in twenty a convenient limit. Convenient, and easy to look up in a table.

related.oneLine: 大量已發表的顯著結果，換一組人重做就做不出來。
related.example: 2015 年，270 位研究者重做 100 個心理學實驗：原研究有 97% 顯著，重做之後只剩 36%。

en.oneLine: A large share of published significant findings do not come back when someone else runs the study again.
en.example: In 2015, 270 researchers redid 100 psychology experiments: 97% of the originals were significant, 36% of the replications were.

en.oneLine: Adjusting the analysis while watching the result — a few more subjects, another covariate, another outcome — until p drops below the line.
en.example: Simmons and colleagues "showed" in 2011 that hearing "When I'm Sixty-Four" made people a year and a half younger (p = .040). Drop one covariate and p goes back to .33.

simmonsNelsonSimonsohn2011FalsePositive.author: Joseph P. Simmons, Leif D. Nelson, and Uri Simonsohn
simmonsNelsonSimonsohn2011FalsePositive.title: False-Positive Psychology: Undisclosed Flexibility in Data Collection and Analysis Allows Presenting Anything as Significant
simmonsNelsonSimonsohn2011FalsePositive.locator: Study 2：20 名賓州大學大學生分兩組聆聽披頭四〈When I'm Sixty-Four〉或對照曲〈Kalimba〉，再回報出生年月與父親年齡；聽披頭四組經統計校正後平均年齡 20.1 歲，對照組 21.5 歲，F(1,17)=4.92，p=.040。文中另以模擬顯示：容許研究者在資料蒐集與分析上有彈性，可把單一比較的名目 5% 偽陽性率推高到 60.7%。
simmonsNelsonSimonsohn2011FalsePositive.quote: 根據回報的出生日期，聽過〈When I'm Sixty-Four〉的受試者比聽對照曲的受試者年輕了近一歲半（校正後平均 20.1 歲 vs. 21.5 歲），F(1, 17) = 4.92，p = .040。

en.locator: Study 2: 20 University of Pennsylvania undergraduates listened to either the Beatles' "When I'm Sixty-Four" or a control song ("Kalimba"), then reported their birth date and father's age; adjusted mean age was 20.1 in the Beatles condition versus 21.5 in the control condition, F(1,17) = 4.92, p = .040. The paper's simulations separately show that allowing flexibility in data collection and analysis can push a single comparison's nominal 5% false-positive rate up to 60.7%.
en.quote: According to their reported birth date, people were nearly a year-and-a-half younger after listening to "When I'm Sixty-Four" (adjusted M = 20.1 years) rather than to "Kalimba" (adjusted M = 21.5 years), F(1, 17) = 4.92, p = .040.

related.oneLine: 一邊看結果一邊調分析：多收幾個樣本、換個共變量、換個結果變數，直到 p 掉到門檻底下。

en.oneLine: Adjusting the analysis while watching the result — a few more subjects, another covariate, another outcome — until p drops below the line.

en.title: Why It Is Called the Null Hypothesis

replication-crisis.oneLine: 大量已發表的顯著結果，換一組人重做就做不出來。
replication-crisis.example: 2015 年，270 位研究者重做 100 個心理學實驗：原研究有 97% 顯著，重做之後只剩 36%。

related.oneLine: 一邊看結果一邊調分析：多收幾個樣本、換個共變量、換個結果變數，直到 p 掉到門檻底下。
related.example: Simmons 等人 2011 年「證明」聽披頭四的〈When I'm Sixty-Four〉會讓人年輕一歲半（p = .040）。拿掉一個共變量，p 就跳回 .33。

en.oneLine: Adjusting the analysis while watching the result — a few more subjects, another covariate, another outcome — until p drops below the line.
en.example: Simmons and colleagues "showed" in 2011 that hearing "When I'm Sixty-Four" made people a year and a half younger (p = .040). Drop one covariate and p goes back to .33.

related.oneLine: 效果真的存在時，這個研究抓得到它的機率。
related.example: Cohen 在 1962 年算過：那年頭的心理學研究要抓一個中等大小的效果，平均只有 .48 的機會，比丟銅板還差一點。

en.oneLine: The probability that a study will catch an effect that is really there.
en.example: Cohen worked it out in 1962: to catch a medium effect, the psychology studies of the day averaged a .48 chance. Slightly worse than a coin toss.

related.oneLine: 把 Fisher 的證據邏輯和 Neyman-Pearson 的決策邏輯焊成一套固定動作，兩邊的代價都不付。
related.example: Gigerenzer 說這套儀式沒有作者：沒有哪位統計學家願意替它辯護，整個學科卻天天執行。

en.oneLine: Fisher's evidential logic and Neyman-Pearson's decision logic welded into a fixed routine that pays neither one's price.
en.example: Gigerenzer says the ritual has no author: no statistician will defend it, and the field performs it every day.

en.oneLine: A large share of published significant findings do not come back when someone else runs the study again.
en.example: In 2015, 270 researchers redid 100 psychology experiments: 97% of the originals were significant, 36% of the replications were.

openScienceCollaboration2015Reproducibil.author: Open Science Collaboration
openScienceCollaboration2015Reproducibil.title: Estimating the Reproducibility of Psychological Science
openScienceCollaboration2015Reproducibil.locator: 重做 100 篇心理學實證研究（原刊於三份心理學期刊）：原研究中 97% 曾報告達統計顯著（p<.05）；重做後僅 36% 的研究再度達到統計顯著，重做效果量平均只有原研究的一半左右。
openScienceCollaboration2015Reproducibil.quote: Although statistically significant results were reported in 97% of original studies, only 36% of replications yielded statistically significant results.

en.locator: Replicated 100 empirical psychology studies (originally published in three journals): 97% of the original studies had reported statistically significant results (p < .05); only 36% of the replications reached statistical significance, and replication effect sizes were on average about half the magnitude of the original effect sizes.
en.quote: Although statistically significant results were reported in 97% of original studies, only 36% of replications yielded statistically significant results.

en.oneLine: The probability that a study will catch an effect that is really there.

positive-predictive-value.oneLine: 在所有「顯著」的結果裡，真的有效果的佔幾成。它取決於這個領域有多少假說本來就是對的。
positive-predictive-value.example: Ioannidis 2005 的算術就是這一條：假說多半是錯的領域，加上偏低的檢定力，「顯著」結果會有一大半是假的。

en.oneLine: Of everything that comes out "significant," how much is real. It depends on how many hypotheses in the field were true to begin with.
en.example: This is the arithmetic behind Ioannidis 2005: in a field where most hypotheses are false and power is low, most significant findings are false too.

en.title: Why It Is Called the Null Hypothesis

sampling-distribution.oneLine: 同一個統計量在反覆抽樣下會長成的樣子。p 值與信賴區間都是從它身上量出來的。
sampling-distribution.example: 品茶實驗的 70 種挑法就是一個抽樣分佈：全部列出來，再數有幾種跟實際結果一樣極端。

related.oneLine: 假設虛無為真，得到「像這樣或更極端」的結果的機率。
related.example: 八杯茶全挑對，純靠猜的機率是 70 分之 1，約 0.014。這個 0.014 就是 p。

en.oneLine: The probability of a result at least as extreme as the one you got, if the null were true.
en.example: Picking all four milk-first cups by luck alone has probability 1 in 70, about 0.014. That 0.014 is the p value.

related.oneLine: 把標籤重新洗牌，看看純屬巧合能做到多好，再看真實資料排在哪裡。
related.example: 八杯茶就是最小的置換檢定：四杯先倒奶的挑法共 70 種，全對只有 1 種。

en.oneLine: Shuffle the labels, see how well coincidence can do, then see where the real data land.
en.example: Eight cups is the smallest permutation test there is: 70 ways to choose four, exactly one of them all correct.

related.oneLine: 一套會生出區間的規則。95% 是這套規則的長期命中率，不是眼前這一個區間的機率。
related.example: Neyman 1937 自己問：對已經算出來的那一個區間，能不能說真值落在裡面的機率是 95%？他的答案是不能。

en.oneLine: A rule for producing intervals. The 95% is the rule's long-run hit rate, not the probability for the one interval in front of you.
en.example: Neyman asked it himself in 1937: of an interval already computed, may we say the true value lies inside it with probability 95%? His answer was no.

en.oneLine: The shape a statistic takes under repeated sampling. Both p values and confidence intervals are read off it.
en.example: The 70 ways of picking four cups out of eight are a sampling distribution: list them all, then count how many are as extreme as what actually happened.

fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.author: Ronald A. Fisher
fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.title: The Design of Experiments
fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.locator: Ch. II, §5 'The Statement of Experiment', p.13（茶品實驗設定：8 杯，4 杯先加奶）；§8 'The Null Hypothesis'，p.18（1935 初版；部分刷次標為 p.19）。
fisher1935DesignOfExperiments.quote: In relation to any experiment we may speak of this hypothesis as the null hypothesis, and it should be noted that the null hypothesis is never proved or established, but is possibly disproved, in the course of experimentation. Every experiment may be said to exist only in order to give the facts a chance of disproving the null hypothesis.

en.locator: Ch. II §5 'The Statement of Experiment', p.13 (the tea-tasting setup); §8 'The Null Hypothesis', p.18 (1935 first edition; some reprints give p.19).

neymanPearson1933.author: Jerzy Neyman and Egon S. Pearson
neymanPearson1933.title: On the Problem of the Most Efficient Tests of Statistical Hypotheses
neymanPearson1933.container: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London, Series A, vol. 231, pp.289–337
neymanPearson1933.locator: p.291（「rule of behaviour」段落，決策式邏輯的原始表述）；pp.296–297（H0 記號、「errors of the first kind」與第二類錯誤用語首次系統性提出之處）。「power」一詞本身在這篇論文中尚未以此固定術語出現。
neymanPearson1933.quote: If we reject H0, we may reject it when it is true; if we accept H0, we may be accepting it when it is false, that is to say, when really some alternative H_t is true. These two sources of error can rarely be eliminated completely... In trying to choose a proper critical region, we notice at once that it is very easy to control errors of the first kind referred to above.

en.locator: p.291 (the 'rule of behaviour' passage, the original statement of the decision-theoretic logic); pp.296-297 (the notation H0, and the first systematic statement of what became Type I and Type II error). The word 'power' itself is not yet used as a fixed term in this paper.

gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.title: Mindless Statistics
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.container: The Journal of Socio-Economics, vol. 33, no. 5, pp.587–606
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.locator: p.591–592：以金屬板製造商的品管情境為例，逐步示範 Neyman–Pearson 決策邏輯（設定第一類、第二類錯誤率、依成本效益抓樣本數，「接受」不等於「相信」）如何脫胎於工業品管的驗收邏輯。
gigerenzer2004MindlessStatistics.quote: Neyman–Pearson 檢定的典型應用是品質管制。設想一位醫療器材金屬板的製造商：她認為兩類錯誤的代價不對稱——誤報（false alarm）代價較小，漏抓故障（miss）代價較大，因此把第一類錯誤率設得很小、第二類錯誤率設得較大，並據此算出每天須抽驗的樣本數。當她「接受」機器故障這個假設時，她是依此行動、停止生產，但這不代表她相信機器真的故障。

en.locator: pp.591-592: works through a metal-plate manufacturer's quality-control scenario to show how Neyman-Pearson decision logic (setting Type I/II error rates, choosing sample size by cost-benefit trade-off, "accepting" a hypothesis without believing it) grew out of industrial acceptance-sampling logic.
en.quote: A typical application of Neyman–Pearson testing is in quality control. Imagine a manufacturer of metal plates that are used in medical instruments... She considers false alarms, that is, accepting H2 while H1 is true, to be the less serious error, and misses of malfunctioning... to be more serious... She now calculates the required sample size n of plates that must be sampled every day to test the quality of the production... When she accepts H2, she acts as if there were a malfunction and stops production, but this does not mean that she believes that H2 is true.

neymanPearson1933.author: Jerzy Neyman and Egon S. Pearson
neymanPearson1933.title: On the Problem of the Most Efficient Tests of Statistical Hypotheses
neymanPearson1933.container: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London, Series A, vol. 231, pp.289–337
neymanPearson1933.locator: p.291（「rule of behaviour」段落，決策式邏輯的原始表述）；pp.296–297（H0 記號、「errors of the first kind」與第二類錯誤用語首次系統性提出之處）。「power」一詞本身在這篇論文中尚未以此固定術語出現。
neymanPearson1933.quote: If we reject H0, we may reject it when it is true; if we accept H0, we may be accepting it when it is false, that is to say, when really some alternative H_t is true. These two sources of error can rarely be eliminated completely... In trying to choose a proper critical region, we notice at once that it is very easy to control errors of the first kind referred to above.

en.locator: p.291 (the 'rule of behaviour' passage, the original statement of the decision-theoretic logic); pp.296-297 (the notation H0, and the first systematic statement of what became Type I and Type II error). The word 'power' itself is not yet used as a fixed term in this paper.
en.quote: If we reject H0, we may reject it when it is true; if we accept H0, we may be accepting it when it is false, that is to say, when really some alternative H_t is true. These two sources of error can rarely be eliminated completely... In trying to choose a proper critical region, we notice at once that it is very easy to control errors of the first kind referred to above.

significance-level.oneLine: 事前選定的門檻：長期而言，你願意讓多少比例的真虛無被錯判為顯著。
significance-level.example: Fisher 說二十分之一是個方便的界線，如此而已。方便，而且當年好查表。

en.oneLine: A threshold fixed in advance: the share of true nulls you are willing to reject in the long run.
en.example: Fisher called one in twenty a convenient limit. Convenient, and easy to look up in a table.

statistical-power.oneLine: 效果真的存在時，這個研究抓得到它的機率。
statistical-power.example: Cohen 在 1962 年算過：那年頭的心理學研究要抓一個中等大小的效果，平均只有 .48 的機會，比丟銅板還差一點。

en.oneLine: The probability that a study will catch an effect that is really there.
en.example: Cohen worked it out in 1962: to catch a medium effect, the psychology studies of the day averaged a .48 chance. Slightly worse than a coin toss.

en.title: Why It Is Called the Null Hypothesis
